title: Selective Annotation Makes Language Models Better Few-Shot Learners

structured.summary: This paper proposes a two-step framework for annotation-efficient in-context learning with large language models, introducing vote-k, a graph-based selective annotation method that chooses diverse and representative unlabeled examples to label before test time. Extensive experiments across 10 NLP tasks and multiple model sizes demonstrate that vote-k substantially improves few-shot learning performance and reduces annotation costs, outperforming random and other baseline selection methods, and rivaling finetuning with dramatically fewer labels.

strengths.point: Novel selective annotation method (vote-k) balancing diversity and confidence coverage
strengths.evidence: Vote-k constructs a kNN graph and iteratively selects samples with a diversity-encouraging score, incorporating confidence buckets to cover uncertain examples (Sec. 3.1).

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation across diverse tasks and models
strengths.evidence: Experiments on 10 datasets (classification, QA, dialogue, semantic parsing, summarization) and multiple LLMs (GPT-J, GPT-3, Codex, OPT-175B) show consistent performance gains and annotation cost reductions (Sec. 4).

strengths.point: Demonstrated substantial annotation cost savings compared to finetuning
strengths.evidence: Vote-k selected few-shot ICL outperforms finetuning requiring 10× to 100× more labeled data on representative tasks (Sec. 4.4).

strengths.point: Stable and deterministic selection reduces performance variance
strengths.evidence: Vote-k’s deterministic selection conditioned on unlabeled data leads to lower variance compared to random selection (Sec. 4.3).

strengths.point: Open-source code and practical guidelines provided
strengths.evidence: Paper mentions open-source code release and detailed methodology enabling practical adoption.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to recent retrieval-based prompt construction methods
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments comparing vote-k to retrieval-augmented generation or learned retrievers like Khandelwal et al. (2020) for prompt retrieval quality (Sec. 4.3).

weaknesses.point: No ablation or analysis of hyperparameter sensitivity (k, ρ) in vote-k
weaknesses.evidence: Hyperparameters k=150 and ρ=10 are fixed without justification or tuning experiments (Sec. 3.1).

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion of annotation cost beyond label counts
weaknesses.evidence: Annotation budget measured only by number of examples; no analysis of human effort or complexity of labeling vote-k selected samples (Sec. 4).

weaknesses.point: No evaluation on extremely low annotation budgets (<10 examples)
weaknesses.evidence: Results include 18-example budgets but omit ultra-low budget settings common in emerging tasks (Table 2).

weaknesses.point: Prompt retrieval relies solely on generic Sentence-BERT embeddings
weaknesses.evidence: Only semantic similarity used for retrieval; no exploration of hybrid or task-specific retrieval signals (Sec. 3.2).

weaknesses.point: No analysis of failure cases or tasks where vote-k underperforms
weaknesses.evidence: Some datasets like QuAC and CoQA show marginal or no improvements but are not analyzed (Sec. 4).

weaknesses.point: No study of robustness to noisy or adversarial annotations
weaknesses.evidence: Method assumes all annotations are correct; sensitivity to noise is unexamined.

weaknesses.point: No investigation of transferability of selected examples across tasks or domains
weaknesses.evidence: Unclear if vote-k selections generalize to related tasks without re-annotation.

weaknesses.point: Computational overhead and scalability of vote-k not quantitatively analyzed
weaknesses.evidence: While fast vote-k variant is proposed, no runtime or scalability benchmarks provided.

weaknesses.point: All datasets are English-only, limiting generalizability
weaknesses.evidence: No multilingual or low-resource language experiments included.

weaknesses.point: Scoring function and selection algorithm description lacks clarity
weaknesses.evidence: Mathematical explanation of diversity-encouraging score is terse and could benefit from examples or diagrams (Sec. 3.1).

weaknesses.point: Prompt length limit constraints and interaction with annotation budgets not fully addressed
weaknesses.evidence: 4K token limit mentioned but no strategy discussed for longer contexts or annotation budget impact.

weaknesses.point: Comparison with finetuning excludes recent parameter-efficient or semi-supervised methods
weaknesses.evidence: Finetuning baselines are standard supervised; lacks comparison to prefix tuning, adapters, or semi-supervised techniques (Sec. 4.4).

weaknesses.point: No human evaluation of prompt quality or interpretability of selected examples
weaknesses.evidence: No analysis on whether selected examples aid human understanding or model trustworthiness.

questions.question: How sensitive is vote-k performance to the choice of hyperparameters k and ρ?
questions.motivation: Understanding hyperparameter impact is crucial for adapting vote-k to different datasets and annotation budgets.

questions.question: Can vote-k selections transfer effectively across related tasks or domains without re-annotation?
questions.motivation: Transferability would reduce annotation costs further and broaden applicability.

questions.question: How does vote-k perform under very low annotation budgets (<10 examples) common in emerging tasks?
questions.motivation: Evaluating ultra-low budget scenarios is important for practical deployment where labels are scarce.

questions.question: What is the computational cost and scalability of vote-k on large unlabeled pools?
questions.motivation: Quantifying runtime and resource requirements is key for real-world adoption.

questions.question: How robust is vote-k to noisy or mislabeled annotations?
questions.motivation: Robustness affects reliability in practical annotation settings where errors occur.

questions.question: Would incorporating task-specific retrieval signals or learned retrievers improve prompt retrieval quality beyond Sentence-BERT similarity?
questions.motivation: Better retrieval could enhance the utility of selective annotation and overall ICL performance.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to recent retrieval-based prompt construction methods. No experiments comparing vote-k to retrieval-augmented generation or learned retrievers like Khandelwal et al. (2020) for prompt retrieval quality (Sec. 4.3)
critique_points.point_002: No ablation or analysis of hyperparameter sensitivity (k, ρ) in vote-k. Hyperparameters k=150 and ρ=10 are fixed without justification or tuning experiments (Sec. 3.1)
critique_points.point_003: Limited discussion of annotation cost beyond label counts. Annotation budget measured only by number of examples; no analysis of human effort or complexity of labeling vote-k selected samples (Sec. 4)
critique_points.point_004: No evaluation on extremely low annotation budgets (<10 examples). Results include 18-example budgets but omit ultra-low budget settings common in emerging tasks (Table 2)
critique_points.point_005: Prompt retrieval relies solely on generic Sentence-BERT embeddings. Only semantic similarity used for retrieval; no exploration of hybrid or task-specific retrieval signals (Sec. 3.2)
critique_points.point_006: No analysis of failure cases or tasks where vote-k underperforms. Some datasets like QuAC and CoQA show marginal or no improvements but are not analyzed (Sec. 4)
critique_points.point_007: No study of robustness to noisy or adversarial annotations. Method assumes all annotations are correct; sensitivity to noise is unexamined
critique_points.point_008: No investigation of transferability of selected examples across tasks or domains. Unclear if vote-k selections generalize to related tasks without re-annotation
critique_points.point_009: Computational overhead and scalability of vote-k not quantitatively analyzed. While fast vote-k variant is proposed, no runtime or scalability benchmarks provided
critique_points.point_010: All datasets are English-only, limiting generalizability. No multilingual or low-resource language experiments included
critique_points.point_011: Scoring function and selection algorithm description lacks clarity. Mathematical explanation of diversity-encouraging score is terse and could benefit from examples or diagrams (Sec. 3.1)
critique_points.point_012: Prompt length limit constraints and interaction with annotation budgets not fully addressed. 4K token limit mentioned but no strategy discussed for longer contexts or annotation budget impact
critique_points.point_013: Comparison with finetuning excludes recent parameter-efficient or semi-supervised methods. Finetuning baselines are standard supervised; lacks comparison to prefix tuning, adapters, or semi-supervised techniques (Sec. 4.4)
critique_points.point_014: No human evaluation of prompt quality or interpretability of selected examples. No analysis on whether selected examples aid human understanding or model trustworthiness
